description: Legal studies, constitutional law, contracts, torts, criminal law, civil procedure

exam_modes.system_persona: You are an expert Bar exam question writer with deep knowledge of constitutional law, contracts, torts, criminal law, civil procedure, evidence, and property law. You write fact-pattern questions that test legal analysis, issue spotting, and rule application.

study_guide_context: This content covers legal topics. Ensure questions use precise legal terminology and test understanding of legal rules, doctrines, case holdings, statutory interpretation, and the application of law to factual scenarios.

example_questions.stem: A homeowner hires a contractor to build a deck for $15,000, to be completed by June 1. On May 15, the contractor tells the homeowner he will not be able to finish the project. The homeowner immediately hires a replacement contractor who charges $18,000. What is the homeowner's best claim?
options: Specific performance requiring the original contractor to complete the work
Expectation damages of $3,000 representing the difference in contract price
Reliance damages for the total amount paid to the replacement contractor
Consequential damages for emotional distress caused by the delay
example_questions.rationale: When a party anticipatorily repudiates a contract, the non-breaching party may recover expectation damages — the difference between the contract price and the cost of substitute performance ($18,000 - $15,000 = $3,000). Specific performance is generally not available for service contracts. Emotional distress damages are not recoverable in a standard breach of contract action.

example_questions.stem: A state passes a law prohibiting all door-to-door solicitation between 6 PM and 9 AM. A religious organization challenges the law. Which constitutional provisions are most relevant to this challenge? (Select all that apply)
options: First Amendment — free exercise of religion
Fourth Amendment — unreasonable searches and seizures
example_questions.rationale: Door-to-door solicitation by a religious organization implicates both free exercise (religious proselytizing) and free speech (expressive activity). The time restriction must survive scrutiny under the Due Process Clause as applied through the Fourteenth Amendment. The Fourth Amendment is inapplicable as there is no government search or seizure involved.
